thinking.text:
The approach of comparing new commands in the transcript will only capture unique commands (first time each command appears), not repeats. Since 'n', 's', 'e', 'w' appear multiple times, this won't capture the full sequence.

Let me reconsider: I need to track the sequence of commands as they are executed. The best approach is to track the last typing command (or the newest transcript command) and record it each time it changes. This should capture repeats.

But the issue is temporal resolution - at 1fps, multiple commands can be typed between frames. Using the 5fps frames should help.

Let me run the improved_all script to see how many commands we get from all available OCR files, then continue processing more HQ frames.
